topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a desk. Dr. Albright was leaving. My stomach immediately tightened, a cold, unfamiliar knot forming just beneath my ribs. I stared at the screen, the words blurring slightly. A three-month extension, a transition period—standard procedure, I told myself. But the way the message was phrased, the clipped, almost abrupt tone, made the hairs on my arms prickle. I picked up my pen, the cheap plastic feeling slick in my sweaty palm. I didn't feel relief; I felt a sudden, sharp need to check every footnote in my dissertation one more time, as if confirming the foundations were solid before the entire structure might crumble. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning in protest, and began meticulously re-reading the final chapter, searching for any hidden sign of impending abandonment.

[story 2]

"So, he's gone?" I muttered to my roommate, pacing the narrow confines of our cramped dorm room. My voice sounded too loud in the quiet hallway. I didn't look at him; instead, I was running a hand repeatedly through my already messy hair, the motion jerky and agitated. I felt a distinct tension radiating from my shoulders, a tightness that refused to ease. It wasn't sadness, exactly; it was more like a low-grade alarm bell ringing constantly in my head. I glanced at the framed photo of Dr. Albright on my desk—a picture of him smiling, looking impossibly composed. I picked up a textbook, flipped it open to a random page, and started underlining sentences with unnecessary force, trying to impose order on the swirling uncertainty that had taken root in my chest.

[story 3]

I reread the official announcement three times. Each time, the same sense of detachment washed over me, a strange flatness. My fingers tapped a restless rhythm on the edge of my laptop keyboard, a quick, uneven beat that mirrored the erratic thumping inside me. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal against the linoleum floor echoing in the empty office. I walked to the window, looking out at the indifferent campus quad. The autumn light felt harsh, exposing every flaw. I watched a group of students laughing together, their interactions seeming effortless and complete. I didn't feel the urge to call Dr. Albright; instead, I focused intently on organizing my research notes, sorting papers into perfect, sterile piles. It was a distraction, a way to control something that felt entirely out of my grasp.
